title: The 20 Most Intriguing Movies of the Fall and the Best of the Toronto International Film Festival
format: Sean and Amanda run down twenty movies they haven't seen yet, with Toronto correspondent Adam Nayman reporting on a TIFF slate everyone agrees was a little thin this year.

films.title: Do Not Expect Too Much From the End of the World
films.note: Radu Jude's TIFF find, per Nayman "crazy and funny and political." Probably the only movie at the festival with jokes about both Andrew Tate and Ceaușescu.

films.title: Hit Man
films.note: Richard Linklater's Glen Powell assassin comedy. Nayman caught it at Venice; neither host has seen it yet, but it's already high on both their lists.

films.title: Aggro Dr1ft
films.note: Harmony Korine's infrared concert-adjacent film with Travis Scott. Nayman: "Love is too strong a word," but he liked thinking and writing about it more than watching it.

films.title: Seven Veils
films.note: Atom Egoyan's TIFF premiere, which Nayman wrote about for Cinema Scope as a return to his 90s form.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: Jonathan Glazer's Cannes Grand Prix winner. Nayman calls it a major piece of work.

films.title: Last Summer
films.note: Catherine Breillat's Cannes entry, which Nayman liked a lot.

films.title: The Holdovers
films.note: Alexander Payne's dramedy. Nayman: "Beaten to death with a Cat Stevens album," and notes the reviews just replicated the press kit's Hal Ashby comparisons.

films.title: Poolman
films.note: Chris Pine's directorial debut. Word on the street, per Nayman, is that it's unwatchable, which only made him want to see it more.

films.title: Knox Goes Away
films.note: Michael Keaton plays a hitman with accelerating dementia. Nayman was surprised by the warm reception; to him it still "feels like a fake movie."

films.title: Hell of a Summer
films.note: The Finn Wolfhard/Billy Bryk co-directed slasher. Nayman's verdict: "Just not very good."

films.title: When Evil Lurks
films.note: Argentinian horror pickup Nayman calls the best of the Midnight Madness slate.

films.title: Evil Does Not Exist
films.note: Ryusuke Hamaguchi's Venice prizewinner. Nayman's favorite of the festival, a change of pace from all the big-swing Hollywood premieres.

films.title: American Fiction
films.note: Cord Jefferson's directorial debut. Both hosts' pick to win the TIFF Audience Award, and Nayman thinks Jeffrey Wright deserves an Oscar nomination for it.

segments.heading: The 20 most intriguing movies of the fall
segments.note: the ranking system got abandoned mid-taping

films.title: Dream Scenario
films.note: Nicolas Cage as a schlub who starts appearing in everyone's dreams. Amanda ranks it below The Exorcist: Believer, not because it sounds bad, but because it feels like something she's seen before.

films.title: The Exorcist: Believer
films.note: David Gordon Green's fourth entry in the franchise. Kicked off a long digression on whether the devil is real (Sean: no; Amanda's producer Bobby: yes).

films.title: Pain Hustlers
films.note: David Yates directs Chris Evans and Emily Blunt on the opioid crisis. TIFF reviews were bad, and neither host was expecting a searing exposé.

films.title: Next Goal Wins
films.note: Taika Waititi's American Samoa soccer comedy, shot in 2019 and reshot to cut Armie Hammer, with Michael Fassbender starring instead. Reception at TIFF was split.

films.title: Origin
films.note: Ava DuVernay adapts Isabel Wilkerson's Caste by dramatizing Wilkerson's own life, with Aunjanue Ellis in the lead. Mixed reviews out of Venice give Amanda pause.

films.title: Wonka
films.note: Paul King directs Timothée Chalamet in a Roald Dahl prequel that's secretly a musical, which the marketing hasn't shown. Part of why the CinemaCon trailer spooked people.

films.title: Rebel Moon - Part One: A Child of Fire
films.note: Zack Snyder's answer to not getting hired to make Star Wars, for Netflix.

films.title: Taylor Swift: The Eras Tour
films.note: Swift negotiated directly with theater chains and skipped a studio entirely, clearing the October 13th release date around it. Sean is dreading the 10-minute version of "All Too Well"; Amanda is planning her outfit.

films.title: Stop Making Sense
films.note: A24's 4K remaster of the Talking Heads concert film, timed for the same season and now getting a bit overshadowed by the Swift film.

films.title: The Creator
films.note: Gareth Edwards' original sci-fi with John David Washington. Sean wants to support an original big-budget studio film even without knowing yet if it's good.

films.title: Leave the World Behind
films.note: Sam Esmail's second feature, adapting the Rumaan Alam novel with Julia Roberts, Mahershala Ali, and Ethan Hawke. Both hosts are avoiding details before its December 8 Netflix release.

films.title: May December
films.note: Todd Haynes directs Natalie Portman and Julianne Moore in a story loosely inspired by Mary Kay Letourneau, played as arch melodrama.

films.title: Dumb Money
films.note: Craig Gillespie's GameStop-saga movie with Paul Dano and Seth Rogen. Amanda's seeing it that Saturday.

films.title: Anyone But You
films.note: Glen Powell and Sydney Sweeney rom-com whose alleged off-screen chemistry is doing a lot of the promotional lifting. A December release may hinge on the strike ending in time for press.

films.title: The Color Purple
films.note: Blitz Bazawule's musical adaptation, with Fantasia Barrino in her biggest role since American Idol alongside Taraji P. Henson, Danielle Brooks, Colman Domingo, and Halle Bailey.

films.title: The Iron Claw
films.note: Sean Durkin's Von Erich wrestling-family drama with Zac Efron and Jeremy Allen White; Amanda drafted it in the show's movie auction.

films.title: Freud's Last Session
films.note: Anthony Hopkins as Freud opposite Matthew Goode. Barely on either host's radar going in.

films.title: Aquaman and the Lost Kingdom
films.note: A brief honorable mention. DC's in a rough patch right now.

films.title: The Marvels
films.note: Also just an honorable mention alongside Aquaman.

referenced.group: Already seen, left off the ranking
